name: Bob Cranshaw
abstract: Melbourne Robert Cranshaw (December 10, 1932 – November 2, 2016) was an American jazz bassist. His career spanned the heyday of Blue Note Records as a house bassist.

quotes.text: So rather than to get out of it, I got into it. I said okay, I’m not going to fight the union, I have to help the union, but I have to be a liaison between what the union would like to do and what the guys would like to do. And it’s easier for me to go and talk to the jazz musicians or the rock musicians ‘cause they know me, about a situation, and not do anything unless they want me to do it, rather than having a union representative come in and say, you know, you guys have to do that. I don’t like that. My back goes up if they do it to me. So I’m starting to get dialogue going for, we have it for Lincoln Center and we’re trying to do it for the Carnegie Hall Orchestra without disturbing what they already have going. Because we have to take into consideration that the George Weins and the people that are supplying so many weeks of work for these people, so it’s a working — it’s got to be a working thing where it works for the employer and it works for the musicians. But we never had dialogue going between the two vested situations. The union was always going in and saying well you’ve got to do this, and of course when you tell somebody they’ve got to do it their back goes up so you start to fight it. Let’s talk and see what we have coming, how it’s going to benefit the guys before we even think about doing anything. And this is what I’m about. I want to make sure that everybody is going to be covered. Other than that, don’t bother me. I don’t have anything invested and you can’t make my situation work for me through the union then I shouldn’t be there, I should be somewhere else where they work for me. And this is what I’m about as a business.
quotes.caption: Discussing union liaison work at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Bob Cranshaw interviewed by Michael Woods, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Al Gafa
about_quotes.evidence: The piano player was Monty Alexander and the bass player was Bob Cranshaw. And the guitar player was me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Al Gafa interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 2, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Junior Mance Part I
about_quotes.evidence: I had my trio working with Joe, which was Bob Cranshaw and Mickey Roker and myself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Junior Mance Part I interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I have a photo here of the Billy Taylor band... Bob Cranshaw on bass... Yeah, Bob left after a couple of months and he was replaced by Wilbur Bascomb
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Mabern
about_quotes.evidence: we had a good group in Chicago called the MJT + 3, young people. Frank Strozier, Bob Cranshaw, Walter Perkins, myself, Bobby Bryant, you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Mabern interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, March 31, 2010

about_quotes.speaker: Lew Tabackin
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Duke Pearson’s band which was wonderful, because we had a great rhythm section, with Mickey Roker and Bob Cranshaw.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lew Tabackin interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 17, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Wendell Harrison
about_quotes.evidence: And he had Bob Cranshaw, Bob © Fillius Jazz Archive -8- Cunningham and Ben Dixon on drums and on tour instead he got an organ.
